item.title: Knowledge-assisted network via domain features graph fusion for few-shot SAR recognition
item.dc:creator: Kefan Qu
Huanyu Liu
Haipeng Guo
Tianyi Wen
Junbao Li
item.description: Deep learning in Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) recognition has been widely studied, but the recognition models remain significant challenges under sample limitation. For few-shot learning, previous works based on domain features are typically designed for specific scenes, and their reliance on standalone domain features struggles to meet the demands of complex scenarios. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel knowledge-assisted network (KAnet) for few-shot SAR recognition, which mines domain features and employs graph convolution to generate auxiliary knowledge, thereby enhancing the discriminative capability of the vanilla backbone. For insufficient information in few-shot scenarios, we propose a general domain knowledge mining (DKM) module based on the characteristics of SAR images. The module extracts discriminative multi-dimensional features across spatial, statistical, and time-frequency domains. These features are subsequently fused via an autoencoder into a unified, scalable feature representation, thus mitigating the dependence on a single data input in few-shot scenarios. To fully explore the intrinsic relationships among multi-dimensional features, a hierarchical graph convolutional encoder (HGCE) is introduced to adaptively model semantic relationships between feature nodes. By aggregating neighborhood information, the HGCE fuses complementary features and outputs global auxiliary knowledge. This auxiliary knowledge participates in the classification decision together with the vanilla recognition backbone, thereby enhancing the model’s recognition performance under the guidance of physical priors. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (SAR-ACD, FUSAR-Ship, and MSTAR) demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed KAnet method. Published: 2026-07-11T14:58:04+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.810 (must_read)

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134483

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Deep learning in Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) recognition has been widely studied, but the recognition models remain significant challenges under sample limitation. For few-shot learning, previous works based on domain features are typically designed for specific scenes, and their reliance on standalone domain features struggles to meet the demands of complex scenarios. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel knowledge-assisted network (KAnet) for few-shot SAR recognition, which mines domain features and employs graph convolution to generate auxiliary knowledge, thereby enhancing the discriminative capability of the vanilla backbone. For insufficient information in few-shot scenarios, we propose a general domain knowledge mining (DKM) module based on the characteristics of SAR images. The module extracts discriminative multi-dimensional features across spatial, statistical, and time-frequency domains. These features are subsequently fused via an autoencoder into a unified, scalable feature representation, thus mitigating the dependence on a single data input in few-shot scenarios. To fully explore the intrinsic relationships among multi-dimensional features, a hierarchical graph convolutional encoder (HGCE) is introduced to adaptively model semantic relationships between feature nodes. By aggregating neighborhood information, the HGCE fuses complementary features and outputs global auxiliary knowledge. This auxiliary knowledge participates in the classification decision together with the vanilla recognition backbone, thereby enhancing the model’s recognition performance under the guidance of physical priors. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (SAR-ACD, FUSAR-Ship, and MSTAR) demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed KAnet method.

item.title: FreePose: Modeling frequency-decoupled motion trajectories for 3D human pose estimation
item.dc:creator: Xuguang Liu
Yong Wang
Xiaoqing Wang
Wenming Yang
item.description: State-of-the-art 3D human pose estimation methods often rely on time-domain modeling of joint trajectories, yet struggle to distinguish smooth global motion from transient local jitter. To overcome this, we propose FreePose , a fre quency-d e coupled framework that separates motion into low-frequency and high-frequency components via discrete wavelet transform. We design two specialized modules: the Low-Frequency Coefficient Interaction Network (LFCI-net), which captures continuous and smooth motion patterns by applying temporal self-attention to low-frequency coefficients; and the High-Frequency Coefficient Interaction Network (HFCI-net), which extracts fine-grained local motion through multi-scale dilated convolutions and adaptively suppresses noise via learnable gated attention. Compared to time-domain baselines, FreePose reduces MACs by 50%, improves accuracy, and produces smoother motion. Experiments on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP validate the effectiveness of the proposed method under indoor and semi-controlled evaluation settings. Code is available at https://github.com/Lxg-233/FreePose . Published: 2026-07-11T06:33:05+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuguang Liu; Yong Wang; Xiaoqing Wang; Wenming Yang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114423

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

State-of-the-art 3D human pose estimation methods often rely on time-domain modeling of joint trajectories, yet struggle to distinguish smooth global motion from transient local jitter. To overcome this, we propose FreePose , a fre quency-d e coupled framework that separates motion into low-frequency and high-frequency components via discrete wavelet transform. We design two specialized modules: the Low-Frequency Coefficient Interaction Network (LFCI-net), which captures continuous and smooth motion patterns by applying temporal self-attention to low-frequency coefficients; and the High-Frequency Coefficient Interaction Network (HFCI-net), which extracts fine-grained local motion through multi-scale dilated convolutions and adaptively suppresses noise via learnable gated attention. Compared to time-domain baselines, FreePose reduces MACs by 50%, improves accuracy, and produces smoother motion. Experiments on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP validate the effectiveness of the proposed method under indoor and semi-controlled evaluation settings. Code is available at https://github.com/Lxg-233/FreePose .

item.title: Detection transformer with Chain-Of-Thought prompted and Class-Aware Adaptive Query Selection for degenerated image
item.dc:creator: Fei Cai
Zhong Qu
Xuehui Yin
Xuejuan Han
item.description: Object detection techniques have been widely used in traffic safety management and have contributed to the safety of pedestrians and vehicles to a certain extent. Currently, object detection techniques for single types of image degradation have been extensively studied. However, these methods usually require training a separate model for each specific degradation type, which limits the adaptability of object detection technology. To address these issues, we propose a method called CPA-RT-DETR, which is based on RT-DETR and incorporates a Chain-of-Thought Prompted Adaptive (CPA) enhancer with adaptive Fine-grained Channel Attention (FCA). Specifically, the CPA-Enhancer progressively adjusts its enhancement strategy based on the degradation-related information encoded in the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts, thereby improving the detection performance of the model on images with unknown degradation types. Simultaneously, we propose a Bi-directional CNN-based Cross-Scale Feature Fusion (Bi-CCFF) module, which adaptively assigns weights to features according to the varying contributions of multi-resolution features and achieves effective fusion of features across different layers. Subsequently, we construct a Class-Aware Adaptive Query Selection (CAAQS) to initialize the object queries, achieving more precise query instance matching. Experimental results on the RTTS, Cityfoggy, and DAWN datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. Our CPA-RT-DETR-R50 and CPA-RT-DETR-R101 achieve 56.7% and 58.3% mAP on the RTTS dataset, with inference speeds of 23.7 and 15.1 frames per second, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art detection models in terms of accuracy. The code will be released at https://github.com/Caifei123/CPA-RT-DETR . Published: 2026-07-11T06:33:10+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fei Cai; Zhong Qu; Xuehui Yin; Xuejuan Han

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114432

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Object detection techniques have been widely used in traffic safety management and have contributed to the safety of pedestrians and vehicles to a certain extent. Currently, object detection techniques for single types of image degradation have been extensively studied. However, these methods usually require training a separate model for each specific degradation type, which limits the adaptability of object detection technology. To address these issues, we propose a method called CPA-RT-DETR, which is based on RT-DETR and incorporates a Chain-of-Thought Prompted Adaptive (CPA) enhancer with adaptive Fine-grained Channel Attention (FCA). Specifically, the CPA-Enhancer progressively adjusts its enhancement strategy based on the degradation-related information encoded in the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts, thereby improving the detection performance of the model on images with unknown degradation types. Simultaneously, we propose a Bi-directional CNN-based Cross-Scale Feature Fusion (Bi-CCFF) module, which adaptively assigns weights to features according to the varying contributions of multi-resolution features and achieves effective fusion of features across different layers. Subsequently, we construct a Class-Aware Adaptive Query Selection (CAAQS) to initialize the object queries, achieving more precise query instance matching. Experimental results on the RTTS, Cityfoggy, and DAWN datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. Our CPA-RT-DETR-R50 and CPA-RT-DETR-R101 achieve 56.7% and 58.3% mAP on the RTTS dataset, with inference speeds of 23.7 and 15.1 frames per second, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art detection models in terms of accuracy. The code will be released at https://github.com/Caifei123/CPA-RT-DETR .

item.title: Frozen-fusion network with spatial-temporal learning for video action recognition
item.dc:creator: Zhancheng Zhang
Zuxi Zhang
Wenhao Tao
Xiaoqing Luo
Fuyuan Hu
item.description: Due to the high computational cost of fully fine-tuning video models, recent research focuses on efficiently transferring large image models to video understanding tasks. Vision Transformers (ViTs) commonly leverage image pretraining followed by finetuning, achieving strong performance in video action recognition. Although recent fine-tuning methods reduce computational cost, these approaches often struggle to capture multi-scale temporal dynamics, resulting in suboptimal performance in complex action recognition scenarios. To address this problem, we propose a Frozen-Fusion network with spatial–temporal learning for video action recognition. Specifically, the proposed network comprises a frozen CLIP image encoder (CLIP-ViT) and a trainable spatial–temporal network (ST-Net). A gated fusion mechanism aligns patch tokens from each CLIP-ViT layer with the corresponding hierarchical features in ST-Net, enabling adaptive integration of multi-level spatial representations without backpropagation through the frozen backbone. To capture both short- and long-term temporal dependencies, a novel Temporal Convolution and Attention Fusion (TCAF) module is introduced and integrated into ST-Net for effective temporal modeling. For the [CLS] token outputs from each CLIP-ViT layer, a Temporal Shift Multi-Scale Gate Convolution (TSMGC) module is proposed to perform temporal modeling, with the resulting features injected into the corresponding layers of ST-Net to enhance its temporal representation. Experiments on the Something-Something V1 and V2 datasets show that our proposed network achieves competitive performance with 63.7% and 74.1% Top-1 accuracy using a 16-frame sampling strategy with a CLIP-ViT-L/14 pre-trained backbone, respectively. Experimental results show that our approach significantly improves action recognition in complex temporal scenes while efficiently utilizing the spatial representation capabilities of CLIP. Published: 2026-07-11T14:51:26+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.762 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhancheng Zhang; Zuxi Zhang; Wenhao Tao; Xiaoqing Luo; Fuyuan Hu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114414

Relevance: 0.762 (consider)

Due to the high computational cost of fully fine-tuning video models, recent research focuses on efficiently transferring large image models to video understanding tasks. Vision Transformers (ViTs) commonly leverage image pretraining followed by finetuning, achieving strong performance in video action recognition. Although recent fine-tuning methods reduce computational cost, these approaches often struggle to capture multi-scale temporal dynamics, resulting in suboptimal performance in complex action recognition scenarios. To address this problem, we propose a Frozen-Fusion network with spatial–temporal learning for video action recognition. Specifically, the proposed network comprises a frozen CLIP image encoder (CLIP-ViT) and a trainable spatial–temporal network (ST-Net). A gated fusion mechanism aligns patch tokens from each CLIP-ViT layer with the corresponding hierarchical features in ST-Net, enabling adaptive integration of multi-level spatial representations without backpropagation through the frozen backbone. To capture both short- and long-term temporal dependencies, a novel Temporal Convolution and Attention Fusion (TCAF) module is introduced and integrated into ST-Net for effective temporal modeling. For the [CLS] token outputs from each CLIP-ViT layer, a Temporal Shift Multi-Scale Gate Convolution (TSMGC) module is proposed to perform temporal modeling, with the resulting features injected into the corresponding layers of ST-Net to enhance its temporal representation. Experiments on the Something-Something V1 and V2 datasets show that our proposed network achieves competitive performance with 63.7% and 74.1% Top-1 accuracy using a 16-frame sampling strategy with a CLIP-ViT-L/14 pre-trained backbone, respectively. Experimental results show that our approach significantly improves action recognition in complex temporal scenes while efficiently utilizing the spatial representation capabilities of CLIP.

item.title: VERSE: Visual Embedding Reduction and Space Exploration — Latent-space clustering for improving document understanding
item.dc:creator: Ignacio de Rodrigo
Alvaro J. Lopez-Lopez
Jaime Boal
item.description: Conventionally, synthetic training data quality is evaluated through human perception, prioritizing visual realism. From the model’s perspective, what truly matters is whether a sample lies within the right region of its embedding space. This work introduces VERSE, a methodology for analyzing and improving the performance of Vision–Language Models by exploring their visual embedding space. VERSE enables the visualization of latent representations to assess model feasibility, identifies problematic regions, and guides synthetic data generation to enhance performance in those clusters. We validate the proposed methodology for Visually-rich Document Understanding by training on the synthetic MERIT Dataset and evaluating on its real-world counterpart, MERIT Secret, focusing on key information extraction as a sequence-generation task scoped to transcripts of records in Spanish. Results show that VERSE uncovers the visual features associated with error-prone clusters, and that retraining with samples containing these features substantially boosts F1 performance without degrading generalization. On-premise models optimized with VERSE—Donut (F1 = 0.76) and Idefics2 (F1 = 0.81)—match or surpass SaaS solutions such as GPT-4o (F1 = 0.78) and Pixtral (F1 = 0.73), preserving data privacy and avoiding external APIs. Published: 2026-07-11T06:33:22+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.759 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ignacio de Rodrigo; Alvaro J. Lopez-Lopez; Jaime Boal

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114448

Relevance: 0.759 (consider)

Conventionally, synthetic training data quality is evaluated through human perception, prioritizing visual realism. From the model’s perspective, what truly matters is whether a sample lies within the right region of its embedding space. This work introduces VERSE, a methodology for analyzing and improving the performance of Vision–Language Models by exploring their visual embedding space. VERSE enables the visualization of latent representations to assess model feasibility, identifies problematic regions, and guides synthetic data generation to enhance performance in those clusters. We validate the proposed methodology for Visually-rich Document Understanding by training on the synthetic MERIT Dataset and evaluating on its real-world counterpart, MERIT Secret, focusing on key information extraction as a sequence-generation task scoped to transcripts of records in Spanish. Results show that VERSE uncovers the visual features associated with error-prone clusters, and that retraining with samples containing these features substantially boosts F1 performance without degrading generalization. On-premise models optimized with VERSE—Donut (F1 = 0.76) and Idefics2 (F1 = 0.81)—match or surpass SaaS solutions such as GPT-4o (F1 = 0.78) and Pixtral (F1 = 0.73), preserving data privacy and avoiding external APIs.

item.title: Learning group collaboration for efficient multi-agent reinforcement learning
item.dc:creator: Qide Wang
Daxin Liu
Zhenyu Liu
Jianrong Tan
item.description: Sequence modeling provides a self-attention mechanism-guided full-scale communication for multi-agent decision-making, showing potential in cooperative tasks. However, message sharing among all agents involves redundant observations and computational overhead. When dealing with complex multi-agent systems, it becomes difficult to distinguish valuable information for collaboration under limited exploration, leading to poor action prediction. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named group-based Multi-Agent Transformer (g-MAT). Guided by the role-discovery mechanisms, it constructs a compressed communication topology and achieves an efficient sequential policy. Specifically, the agents are divided into several role-labeled groups according to the behavioral effects. Then, the hybrid attention layer is designed to model the high-level interrelationships among agents based on the grouping scheme, where the local information from teammates and global information from other groups helps to promote collaboration. Extensive experiments on the challenging benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of g-MAT across a variety of cooperative tasks, where agents are able to aggregate into explainable groups with less manual assistance and task priors. Published: 2026-07-11T06:37:30+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.757 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qide Wang; Daxin Liu; Zhenyu Liu; Jianrong Tan

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116612

Relevance: 0.757 (consider)

Sequence modeling provides a self-attention mechanism-guided full-scale communication for multi-agent decision-making, showing potential in cooperative tasks. However, message sharing among all agents involves redundant observations and computational overhead. When dealing with complex multi-agent systems, it becomes difficult to distinguish valuable information for collaboration under limited exploration, leading to poor action prediction. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named group-based Multi-Agent Transformer (g-MAT). Guided by the role-discovery mechanisms, it constructs a compressed communication topology and achieves an efficient sequential policy. Specifically, the agents are divided into several role-labeled groups according to the behavioral effects. Then, the hybrid attention layer is designed to model the high-level interrelationships among agents based on the grouping scheme, where the local information from teammates and global information from other groups helps to promote collaboration. Extensive experiments on the challenging benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of g-MAT across a variety of cooperative tasks, where agents are able to aggregate into explainable groups with less manual assistance and task priors.

item.title: Scaling Up Forest Vision with Synthetic Data
item.dc:creator: Yihang She
Andrew Blake
David Coomes
Srinivasan Keshav
item.description: Abstract Accurate tree segmentation is a key step in extracting individual tree metrics from forest laser scans, and is essential to understanding ecosystem functions in carbon cycling and beyond. Over the past decade, tree segmentation algorithms have advanced rapidly due to developments in AI. However, existing public 3D forest datasets are not large enough to build robust tree segmentation systems. Motivated by the success of synthetic data in other domains such as self-driving, we investigate whether similar approaches can help with tree segmentation. In place of expensive field data collection and annotation, we use synthetic data during pretraining, and then require only minimal, real forest plot annotation for fine-tuning. We have developed Cambridge Arboreal Modelling Panoptic 3D (CAMP3D), a new synthetic data generation pipeline to do this for forest vision tasks, integrating advances in game engines with physics-based LiDAR simulation. Using CAMP3D, we have produced a comprehensive, diverse, annotated 3D forest dataset on an unprecedented scale. Extensive experiments with a state-of-the-art tree segmentation algorithm and a popular real dataset show that our synthetic data can substantially reduce the need for labelled real data. After fine-tuning on just a single, real, forest plot of less than 0.1 hectare, the pretrained model achieves segmentations that are competitive with a model trained on the full scale real data. We have also identified critical factors for successful use of synthetic data: physics, diversity, and scale, paving the way for more robust 3D forest vision systems in the future. Our CAMP3D pipeline and the resulting dataset are available at https://github.com/yihshe/CAMP3D.git . Published: 2026-07-11T06:27:49+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.755 (consider)

Abstract Accurate tree segmentation is a key step in extracting individual tree metrics from forest laser scans, and is essential to understanding ecosystem functions in carbon cycling and beyond. Over the past decade, tree segmentation algorithms have advanced rapidly due to developments in AI. However, existing public 3D forest datasets are not large enough to build robust tree segmentation systems. Motivated by the success of synthetic data in other domains such as self-driving, we investigate whether similar approaches can help with tree segmentation. In place of expensive field data collection and annotation, we use synthetic data during pretraining, and then require only minimal, real forest plot annotation for fine-tuning. We have developed Cambridge Arboreal Modelling Panoptic 3D (CAMP3D), a new synthetic data generation pipeline to do this for forest vision tasks, integrating advances in game engines with physics-based LiDAR simulation. Using CAMP3D, we have produced a comprehensive, diverse, annotated 3D forest dataset on an unprecedented scale. Extensive experiments with a state-of-the-art tree segmentation algorithm and a popular real dataset show that our synthetic data can substantially reduce the need for labelled real data. After fine-tuning on just a single, real, forest plot of less than 0.1 hectare, the pretrained model achieves segmentations that are competitive with a model trained on the full scale real data. We have also identified critical factors for successful use of synthetic data: physics, diversity, and scale, paving the way for more robust 3D forest vision systems in the future. Our CAMP3D pipeline and the resulting dataset are available at https://github.com/yihshe/CAMP3D.git .

item.title: Towards Stable Source-Free Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Dong Zhao
Qi Zang
Nan Pu
Jinlong Li
Shuang Wang
Nicu Sebe
Zhun Zhong
item.description: We focus on Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) in semantic segmentation, which aims to adapt a source-trained model to a target domain without accessing source data. Self-training with a teacher-student framework is a common solution, but it often suffers from error accumulation caused by noisy pseudo-labels, leading to poor adaptability and unstable training. We analyze this issue and identify two key observations: (I) samples with high stability consistently achieve high accuracy, while unstable samples are the main source of errors; (II) stable self-training requires the teacher to incorporate improved students over time. Motivated by these insights, we propo‘se a novel Stable Self-Training (Stable-ST) framework, which consists of Stable Neighbor Denoising (SND) to correct pseudo-labels via bi-level neighbor-guided learning, and an enhanced Dynamic Teacher Update+ (DTU+) mechanism that leverages a new feedback metric, Neighbor Feature Density (NFD), to ensure reliable teacher updates. Furthermore, we provide a theoretical convergence analysis of SND, offering formal justification for its stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Stable-ST achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse SFDA settings, including traditional and continual urban adaptation, as well as medical and remote sensing scenarios. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/DZhaoXd/Stable-ST . Published: 2026-07-11T14:55:57+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.755 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dong Zhao; Qi Zang; Nan Pu; Jinlong Li; Shuang Wang; Nicu Sebe; Zhun Zhong

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02929-6

Relevance: 0.755 (consider)

We focus on Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) in semantic segmentation, which aims to adapt a source-trained model to a target domain without accessing source data. Self-training with a teacher-student framework is a common solution, but it often suffers from error accumulation caused by noisy pseudo-labels, leading to poor adaptability and unstable training. We analyze this issue and identify two key observations: (I) samples with high stability consistently achieve high accuracy, while unstable samples are the main source of errors; (II) stable self-training requires the teacher to incorporate improved students over time. Motivated by these insights, we propo‘se a novel Stable Self-Training (Stable-ST) framework, which consists of Stable Neighbor Denoising (SND) to correct pseudo-labels via bi-level neighbor-guided learning, and an enhanced Dynamic Teacher Update+ (DTU+) mechanism that leverages a new feedback metric, Neighbor Feature Density (NFD), to ensure reliable teacher updates. Furthermore, we provide a theoretical convergence analysis of SND, offering formal justification for its stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Stable-ST achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse SFDA settings, including traditional and continual urban adaptation, as well as medical and remote sensing scenarios. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/DZhaoXd/Stable-ST .

item.title: Shape-aware unsupervised optical flow via multi-scale recurrent refinement and texture-aware loss
item.dc:creator: Ziyang Zhou
Zemin Cai
Guanhong Huang
Jian-huang Lai
Dagang Zheng
item.description: Unsupervised optical flow has made significant progress, yet advanced iterative frameworks such as RAFT remain underexplored due to high computational cost in training and inference. To address this, we propose a lightweight recurrent architecture with multi-scale receptive field modules that capture both global context and local motion. This design reduces the number of refinement iterations while maintaining accuracy, improving efficiency. We further introduce a texture-aware AD-Census loss that adaptively balances Charbonnier and Census terms based on local texture, providing more stable supervision in challenging regions. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance among end-to-end unsupervised approaches, with improved boundary preservation and motion coherence. The source code is available at https://github.com/Zemin-Cai/SAUnsupervisedOF . Published: 2026-07-11T14:51:58+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.753 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ziyang Zhou; Zemin Cai; Guanhong Huang; Jian-huang Lai; Dagang Zheng

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114425

Relevance: 0.753 (consider)

Unsupervised optical flow has made significant progress, yet advanced iterative frameworks such as RAFT remain underexplored due to high computational cost in training and inference. To address this, we propose a lightweight recurrent architecture with multi-scale receptive field modules that capture both global context and local motion. This design reduces the number of refinement iterations while maintaining accuracy, improving efficiency. We further introduce a texture-aware AD-Census loss that adaptively balances Charbonnier and Census terms based on local texture, providing more stable supervision in challenging regions. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance among end-to-end unsupervised approaches, with improved boundary preservation and motion coherence. The source code is available at https://github.com/Zemin-Cai/SAUnsupervisedOF .

item.title: Discriminative Relation-Aware Data-Free Adversarial Distillation
item.dc:creator: Xingpeng Yao
Renjie Huang
Jianping Gou
Lan Du
Qing Tian
Shaoning Zeng
item.description: Data-free knowledge distillation (DFKD) aims to transfer knowledge from a pretrained teacher to a compact student without access to the original training data. Existing DFKD methods are fundamentally limited by two factors: the absence of explicit structural constraints in synthetic data generation, and the reliance on convolutional teachers whose inductive biases restrict supervision to local patterns. We argue that effective data-free distillation requires both discriminative relational structure among samples and global semantic guidance across feature representations. To this end, we propose RDAD (Relation-aware Data-Free Adversarial Distillation), a principled framework that integrates relation-aware adversarial data synthesis with heterogeneous teacher supervision. RDAD introduces a relational triplet objective with distance-aware hard negative sampling to enforce intra-class compactness and inter-class separability in the teacher feature space. In addition, a Vision Transformer is incorporated as an auxiliary teacher to provide global contextual supervision that complements the locality bias of convolutional networks, with dynamically balanced contributions during adversarial distillation. This unified formulation enables structurally coherent synthetic data generation and more expressive knowledge transfer in the absence of real data. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RDAD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-free distillation methods, particularly for low-capacity students. Published: 2026-07-11T06:37:31+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.748 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xingpeng Yao; Renjie Huang; Jianping Gou; Lan Du; Qing Tian; Shaoning Zeng

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133561

Relevance: 0.748 (consider)

Data-free knowledge distillation (DFKD) aims to transfer knowledge from a pretrained teacher to a compact student without access to the original training data. Existing DFKD methods are fundamentally limited by two factors: the absence of explicit structural constraints in synthetic data generation, and the reliance on convolutional teachers whose inductive biases restrict supervision to local patterns. We argue that effective data-free distillation requires both discriminative relational structure among samples and global semantic guidance across feature representations. To this end, we propose RDAD (Relation-aware Data-Free Adversarial Distillation), a principled framework that integrates relation-aware adversarial data synthesis with heterogeneous teacher supervision. RDAD introduces a relational triplet objective with distance-aware hard negative sampling to enforce intra-class compactness and inter-class separability in the teacher feature space. In addition, a Vision Transformer is incorporated as an auxiliary teacher to provide global contextual supervision that complements the locality bias of convolutional networks, with dynamically balanced contributions during adversarial distillation. This unified formulation enables structurally coherent synthetic data generation and more expressive knowledge transfer in the absence of real data. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RDAD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-free distillation methods, particularly for low-capacity students.

item.title: Discord: Enhancing knowledge distillation via cross-chain of thought and optimal transport alignment between models with different tokenizers
item.dc:creator: Nam Le Hai
Anh Duc Le
Tu Vu
Linh Ngo Van
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve state-of-the-art performance across various NLP tasks but face deployment challenges due to high computational costs and memory constraints. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a promising solution, transferring knowledge from large teacher models to smaller student models. However, existing KD methods often assume shared vocabularies and tokenizers, limiting their flexibility. While approaches like MultiLevelOT, Universal Logit Distillation (ULD) and Dual-Space Knowledge Distillation (DSKD) address vocabulary mismatches, they overlook the critical reasoning-aware distillation aspect. To bridge this gap, we propose Discord , a universal KD framework that integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) augmentation and introduces Cross-CoT Alignment to enhance reasoning transfer. Additionally, we extend Optimal Transport beyond token-wise alignment to a sequence-level and layer-wise alignment approach that adapts to varying sequence lengths while preserving contextual integrity. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Discord outperforms existing KD methods across different vocabulary settings, improving reasoning capabilities and robustness in both domain-specific and cross-task scenarios. Published: 2026-07-11T14:58:30+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.747 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nam Le Hai; Anh Duc Le; Tu Vu; Linh Ngo Van

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116606

Relevance: 0.747 (consider)

Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve state-of-the-art performance across various NLP tasks but face deployment challenges due to high computational costs and memory constraints. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a promising solution, transferring knowledge from large teacher models to smaller student models. However, existing KD methods often assume shared vocabularies and tokenizers, limiting their flexibility. While approaches like MultiLevelOT, Universal Logit Distillation (ULD) and Dual-Space Knowledge Distillation (DSKD) address vocabulary mismatches, they overlook the critical reasoning-aware distillation aspect. To bridge this gap, we propose Discord , a universal KD framework that integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) augmentation and introduces Cross-CoT Alignment to enhance reasoning transfer. Additionally, we extend Optimal Transport beyond token-wise alignment to a sequence-level and layer-wise alignment approach that adapts to varying sequence lengths while preserving contextual integrity. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Discord outperforms existing KD methods across different vocabulary settings, improving reasoning capabilities and robustness in both domain-specific and cross-task scenarios.

item.title: AutoHGNN: Robust and efficient neural architecture search for hypergraph neural networks
item.dc:creator: Sirui Li
Pietro Liò
Xinsheng Li
Baisong Liu
Chengbin Peng
item.description: Hypergraph neural networks have achieved significant success in recent years. However, manual architecture crafting is labor-intensive and often fails to capture complex, higher-order relations, making the automation of hypergraph neural network structure design crucial. To improve the automation and adaptability of hypergraph learning, this paper proposes AutoHGNN, a neural architecture search framework tailored for hypergraph neural networks. First, we introduce a Hyper-Interaction Module (HIM) into the search space to address the mismatch between conventional graph neural network designs and hypergraph data. Second, we propose Hypergraph Stable Topological Distance (HyperSTD) as a structural selection criterion to identify architectures that best preserve the intrinsic structural affinities of the original hypergraph during differentiable search. Extensive experiments on various benchmark datasets demonstrate that AutoHGNN consistently outperforms manually designed and automatically searched baselines in classification accuracy and time efficiency, proving that the discovered architectures are significantly more effective. Published: 2026-07-11T06:37:37+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.746 (consider)

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116609

Relevance: 0.746 (consider)

Hypergraph neural networks have achieved significant success in recent years. However, manual architecture crafting is labor-intensive and often fails to capture complex, higher-order relations, making the automation of hypergraph neural network structure design crucial. To improve the automation and adaptability of hypergraph learning, this paper proposes AutoHGNN, a neural architecture search framework tailored for hypergraph neural networks. First, we introduce a Hyper-Interaction Module (HIM) into the search space to address the mismatch between conventional graph neural network designs and hypergraph data. Second, we propose Hypergraph Stable Topological Distance (HyperSTD) as a structural selection criterion to identify architectures that best preserve the intrinsic structural affinities of the original hypergraph during differentiable search. Extensive experiments on various benchmark datasets demonstrate that AutoHGNN consistently outperforms manually designed and automatically searched baselines in classification accuracy and time efficiency, proving that the discovered architectures are significantly more effective.

item.title: An interpretable Depth Map Super-Resolution method via unrolling dual-boundary consistency constrained optimization
item.dc:creator: Lijun Zhao
Hao Ren
Jinjing Zhang
Huihui Bai
Anhong Wang
item.description: Recent Depth Map Super-Resolution (DMSR) methods have incorporated depth edge prediction as auxiliary guidance into optimization models, forming dual-task driven unrolled networks for edge refinement. However, these methods either neglect explicit dual-boundary consistency constraints or fuse color and depth boundaries only once, leading to degraded generalization and inferior performance. To address this, we formulate the DMSR problem as an explicit dual-boundary consistency-constrained triple-task optimization model. Based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM), the proposed model is cast as an iterative sub-optimization process involving color-edge, depth-edge, depth map, and augmented Lagrange multiplier updates, which are subsequently unrolled into an interpretable ADMM network. Within this architecture, we embed learnable modules into the algorithmic unfolding to achieve high-throughput transmission, thereby boosting representation capability. Furthermore, we propose a Multi-cross Association-compensation Fusion (MAF) module to approximate the proximal operator, progressively aggregating color-edge, depth-edge, and auxiliary-variable features to guide depth map update. Evaluations on the representative Middlebury RGB-D and Lu RGB-D datasets demonstrate that the proposed method surpasses most mainstream methods, such as PMBANet, AMHF, HCGNet, EC-DSRNet, etc. Our codes will be publicly released at https://github.com/mdcnn/EDC-Net . Published: 2026-07-11T14:51:53+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.745 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lijun Zhao; Hao Ren; Jinjing Zhang; Huihui Bai; Anhong Wang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114424

Relevance: 0.745 (consider)

Recent Depth Map Super-Resolution (DMSR) methods have incorporated depth edge prediction as auxiliary guidance into optimization models, forming dual-task driven unrolled networks for edge refinement. However, these methods either neglect explicit dual-boundary consistency constraints or fuse color and depth boundaries only once, leading to degraded generalization and inferior performance. To address this, we formulate the DMSR problem as an explicit dual-boundary consistency-constrained triple-task optimization model. Based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM), the proposed model is cast as an iterative sub-optimization process involving color-edge, depth-edge, depth map, and augmented Lagrange multiplier updates, which are subsequently unrolled into an interpretable ADMM network. Within this architecture, we embed learnable modules into the algorithmic unfolding to achieve high-throughput transmission, thereby boosting representation capability. Furthermore, we propose a Multi-cross Association-compensation Fusion (MAF) module to approximate the proximal operator, progressively aggregating color-edge, depth-edge, and auxiliary-variable features to guide depth map update. Evaluations on the representative Middlebury RGB-D and Lu RGB-D datasets demonstrate that the proposed method surpasses most mainstream methods, such as PMBANet, AMHF, HCGNet, EC-DSRNet, etc. Our codes will be publicly released at https://github.com/mdcnn/EDC-Net .

item.title: DPLoRA: Stable Diffusion Model distillation via Dynamic Parallel LoRA branches
item.dc:creator: Weibin Zeng
Yan Kong
Fuzhang Wu
Youheng Ren
Sicheng Shen
Yuqing Fan
Weiming Dong
item.description: Industrial applications often employ multiple Stable Diffusion Models (SDMs) to meet diverse user demands. While this approach is effective, it inevitably incurs substantial memory costs and switching overhead. We observe that many SDMs are fine-tuned derivatives of a common base model, and that variations among them stem from parameter updates during domain adaptation—that is, each SDM acquires domain-specific knowledge through a domain-specific gradient subspace. While mapping these subspaces onto lightweight adapters like LoRA offers a promising solution, standard LoRA suffers from fixed-rank constraints and requires heuristic manual tuning, limiting its ability to capture domain knowledge. Therefore, we present D ynamic P arallel LoRA ( DPLoRA ), a lightweight adaptation framework built upon LoRA principles. DPLoRA distills domain knowledge from fine-tuned SDMs by automatically identifying their domain gradients while minimizing inference overhead. Specifically, DPLoRA dynamically learns the exclusive domain space via parallel LoRA branches with adaptive importance factors and employs a reparameterization strategy to reduce inference memory without performance degradation. The distilled lightweight domain-specialized DPLoRA modules can be seamlessly plugged into a single general base model, enabling efficient handling of diverse inputs by swapping the corresponding DPLoRA modules. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and widely-used fine-tuned models demonstrate the practicality and effectiveness of our proposed method. Published: 2026-07-11T14:52:02+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.742 (consider)

Authors: Weibin Zeng; Yan Kong; Fuzhang Wu; Youheng Ren; Sicheng Shen; Yuqing Fan; Weiming Dong

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114420

Relevance: 0.742 (consider)

Industrial applications often employ multiple Stable Diffusion Models (SDMs) to meet diverse user demands. While this approach is effective, it inevitably incurs substantial memory costs and switching overhead. We observe that many SDMs are fine-tuned derivatives of a common base model, and that variations among them stem from parameter updates during domain adaptation—that is, each SDM acquires domain-specific knowledge through a domain-specific gradient subspace. While mapping these subspaces onto lightweight adapters like LoRA offers a promising solution, standard LoRA suffers from fixed-rank constraints and requires heuristic manual tuning, limiting its ability to capture domain knowledge. Therefore, we present D ynamic P arallel LoRA ( DPLoRA ), a lightweight adaptation framework built upon LoRA principles. DPLoRA distills domain knowledge from fine-tuned SDMs by automatically identifying their domain gradients while minimizing inference overhead. Specifically, DPLoRA dynamically learns the exclusive domain space via parallel LoRA branches with adaptive importance factors and employs a reparameterization strategy to reduce inference memory without performance degradation. The distilled lightweight domain-specialized DPLoRA modules can be seamlessly plugged into a single general base model, enabling efficient handling of diverse inputs by swapping the corresponding DPLoRA modules. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and widely-used fine-tuned models demonstrate the practicality and effectiveness of our proposed method.

item.title: Brain-TVR: A multi-granularity structure-aware text-video retrieval framework inspired by human brain episodic memory neural mechanisms
item.dc:creator: Yichen Liu
Huan Rong
Zhenyu Lu
Zhifei Li
Canyang Liu
Heng Liu
item.description: Text-Video Retrieval serves as a difficult challenge in multimedia research, striving to bridge visual content in videos with natural language descriptions and garnering growing attention in recent years. Current advanced methods achieve multi-granular alignment between text and video, performing not only coarse-grained global representation but also fine-grained local detail extraction. However, these approaches often fail to model relationships across multi-scale elements, making it difficult to distinguish videos that share similar components but differ in compositional structure. Consequently, semantic nuances arising from varied combinations of these elements remain underrepresented, limiting retrieval precision. Inspired by the human brain’s episodic memory pathway, we propose a novel Text-Video Retrieval framework called Brain-TVR, which not only captures multi-granular elements but also organizes them into a hierarchical structure and map them into a Poincaré ball to further boost retrieval accuracy. Furthermore, we design a two-stage retrieval strategy that improves efficiency and boosts generalization ability. Experimental results on various benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach, with Brain-TVR achieving best retrieval precision, efficiency and generalization across all benchmarks. Visualization experiments confirm that our hierarchical multi-granular representations can effectively capture complex scene structures, with compositional hard-negative evaluation, robustness analysis, as well as hyperparameter sensitivity study further validate the effectiveness and generalization of our approach. Published: 2026-07-11T15:30:05+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.740 (consider)

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134480

Relevance: 0.740 (consider)

Text-Video Retrieval serves as a difficult challenge in multimedia research, striving to bridge visual content in videos with natural language descriptions and garnering growing attention in recent years. Current advanced methods achieve multi-granular alignment between text and video, performing not only coarse-grained global representation but also fine-grained local detail extraction. However, these approaches often fail to model relationships across multi-scale elements, making it difficult to distinguish videos that share similar components but differ in compositional structure. Consequently, semantic nuances arising from varied combinations of these elements remain underrepresented, limiting retrieval precision. Inspired by the human brain’s episodic memory pathway, we propose a novel Text-Video Retrieval framework called Brain-TVR, which not only captures multi-granular elements but also organizes them into a hierarchical structure and map them into a Poincaré ball to further boost retrieval accuracy. Furthermore, we design a two-stage retrieval strategy that improves efficiency and boosts generalization ability. Experimental results on various benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach, with Brain-TVR achieving best retrieval precision, efficiency and generalization across all benchmarks. Visualization experiments confirm that our hierarchical multi-granular representations can effectively capture complex scene structures, with compositional hard-negative evaluation, robustness analysis, as well as hyperparameter sensitivity study further validate the effectiveness and generalization of our approach.

item.title: MaTe3D: Mask-Guided Text-based 3D-Aware Portrait Editing
item.dc:creator: Kangneng Zhou
Daiheng Gao
Xuan Wang
Jie Zhang
Peng Zhang
Xusen Sun
Longhao Zhang
Shiqi Yang
Bang Zhang
Liefeng Bo
Yaxing Wang
Ming-Ming Cheng
item.description: 3D-aware portrait editing has a wide range of applications in multiple fields. However, current approaches are limited due that they can only perform mask-guided or text-based editing. Even by fusing the two procedures into a model, the editing quality and stability cannot be ensured. To address this limitation, we propose MaTe3D: mask-guided text-based 3D-aware portrait editing. In this framework, first, we introduce a new SDF-based 3D generator which learns local and global representations with proposed SDF and density consistency losses. This enhances masked-based editing in local areas; second, we present a novel distillation strategy: Conditional Distillation on Geometry and Texture (CDGT). Compared to exiting distillation strategies, it mitigates visual ambiguity and avoids mismatch between texture and geometry, thereby producing stable texture and convincing geometry while editing. Additionally, we create the CatMask-HQ dataset, a large-scale high-resolution cat face annotation for exploration of model generalization and expansion. We perform expensive experiments on both the FFHQ and CatMask-HQ datasets to demonstrate the editing quality and stability of the proposed method. Our method faithfully generates a 3D-aware edited face image based on a modified mask and a text prompt. Our code and models will be publicly at https://github.com/MontaEllis/MaTe3D . Published: 2026-07-12T14:52:05+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.739 (ignore)

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02916-x

3D-aware portrait editing has a wide range of applications in multiple fields. However, current approaches are limited due that they can only perform mask-guided or text-based editing. Even by fusing the two procedures into a model, the editing quality and stability cannot be ensured. To address this limitation, we propose MaTe3D: mask-guided text-based 3D-aware portrait editing. In this framework, first, we introduce a new SDF-based 3D generator which learns local and global representations with proposed SDF and density consistency losses. This enhances masked-based editing in local areas; second, we present a novel distillation strategy: Conditional Distillation on Geometry and Texture (CDGT). Compared to exiting distillation strategies, it mitigates visual ambiguity and avoids mismatch between texture and geometry, thereby producing stable texture and convincing geometry while editing. Additionally, we create the CatMask-HQ dataset, a large-scale high-resolution cat face annotation for exploration of model generalization and expansion. We perform expensive experiments on both the FFHQ and CatMask-HQ datasets to demonstrate the editing quality and stability of the proposed method. Our method faithfully generates a 3D-aware edited face image based on a modified mask and a text prompt. Our code and models will be publicly at https://github.com/MontaEllis/MaTe3D .

item.title: Learning cross-modal semantic consistency and complementary fusion for condition recognition in zinc oxide rotary kilns
item.dc:creator: Chengzhen Ning
Xiaoxu Han
Chaobo Zhang
Jinghui Zhong
Yan Liu
Xiaoming Wang
Xiaojun Liang
Weihua Gui
item.description: Reliable condition recognition is essential for zinc oxide rotary kilns to support process monitoring and stable production. Although flame images and process measurements provide complementary information, their joint utilization is hindered by cross-modal semantic inconsistency and distributional discrepancies. Existing studies often rely on single-modal learning or coarse fusion, which limits recognition reliability in complex industrial environments. To address this issue, this paper proposes DMSAF, a decoupled multi-modal method based on semantic alignment and adaptive fusion, for zinc oxide rotary kiln condition recognition. Specifically, a pre-trained CLIP model is employed to extract flame image features, while a cross-variable encoder is designed for DCS data to capture intrinsic coupling relationships among process variables. Unlike conventional strategies that entangle alignment and fusion in a unified process, DMSAF decouples semantic alignment and adaptive fusion into two stages. First, a Cross-modal Semantic Alignment and Reconstruction (CSAR) module reduces semantic and distributional discrepancies through cross-modal knowledge retrieval and intra-modal semantic reconstruction, thereby establishing semantically consistent representations across modalities before fusion. Second, a Semantic Interaction and Adaptive Fusion (SIAF) network models cross-modal dependencies and dynamically integrates complementary information to enhance condition recognition. Extensive experiments on a multi-modal benchmark dataset established under expert knowledge demonstrate that DMSAF achieves superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics and demonstrates strong generalization ability under an additional independent evaluation setting, while maintaining practical inference efficiency for industrial monitoring. Published: 2026-07-11T14:57:58+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.739 (ignore)

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134485

Relevance: 0.739 (ignore)

Reliable condition recognition is essential for zinc oxide rotary kilns to support process monitoring and stable production. Although flame images and process measurements provide complementary information, their joint utilization is hindered by cross-modal semantic inconsistency and distributional discrepancies. Existing studies often rely on single-modal learning or coarse fusion, which limits recognition reliability in complex industrial environments. To address this issue, this paper proposes DMSAF, a decoupled multi-modal method based on semantic alignment and adaptive fusion, for zinc oxide rotary kiln condition recognition. Specifically, a pre-trained CLIP model is employed to extract flame image features, while a cross-variable encoder is designed for DCS data to capture intrinsic coupling relationships among process variables. Unlike conventional strategies that entangle alignment and fusion in a unified process, DMSAF decouples semantic alignment and adaptive fusion into two stages. First, a Cross-modal Semantic Alignment and Reconstruction (CSAR) module reduces semantic and distributional discrepancies through cross-modal knowledge retrieval and intra-modal semantic reconstruction, thereby establishing semantically consistent representations across modalities before fusion. Second, a Semantic Interaction and Adaptive Fusion (SIAF) network models cross-modal dependencies and dynamically integrates complementary information to enhance condition recognition. Extensive experiments on a multi-modal benchmark dataset established under expert knowledge demonstrate that DMSAF achieves superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics and demonstrates strong generalization ability under an additional independent evaluation setting, while maintaining practical inference efficiency for industrial monitoring.

item.title: SVR-UNet: Frequency-Aware View-Routed Analysis-Synthesis Sampling for 3D Medical Image Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Shuanghua Ye
Wenwen Tang
Zhao Huang
Huiyu Zhou
Jin Liu
Zhiwen Zheng
Xiaoshuai Zhang
Xingru Huang
item.description: Accurate 3D medical image segmentation remains challenging for anisotropic clinical volumes and fine anatomical structures, where conventional encoder-decoder sampling often causes aliasing, boundary attenuation, and unstable detail reconstruction. In this work, we argue that the bottleneck is not only representation capacity, but also how information is discarded and reconstructed across scales. We propose SVR-UNet, a frequency-aware and view-routed U-shaped network that reformulates downsampling and upsampling as an explicit analysis-synthesis process. Specifically, we introduce Trihedral Spectro-Projective Decimation (TSPD) and Trihedral Spectro-Projective Prolongation (TSPP) as sampling modules that adapt established ideas from anti-aliased filtering, wavelet multiresolution analysis, and orthogonal-view modeling to encoder–decoder resolution transitions. TSPD performs anti-aliased decimation, tri-view projective decomposition, Haar wavelet subband modeling, and gradient-prior view routing to preserve boundary-critical high-frequency content during encoding. TSPP performs controlled spectral synthesis during decoding by combining tri-view wavelet-lifting reconstruction with edge-confidence-guided fusion, enabling anatomically faithful recovery of thin and weak-contrast structures while suppressing upsampling artifacts. Extensive experiments on 10 public 3D medical image segmentation benchmarks against 26 recent state-of-the-art (SoTA) methods show that SVR-UNet obtains competitive overall results and improves several boundary-oriented metrics, while maintaining a compact footprint of 16.88M parameters and 166.99G FLOPs. The results suggest that frequency-aware, view-routed sampling can serve as a useful complement to backbone scaling for volumetric segmentation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/IMOP-lab/SVR-UNet . Published: 2026-07-11T14:59:04+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.738 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuanghua Ye; Wenwen Tang; Zhao Huang; Huiyu Zhou; Jin Liu; Zhiwen Zheng; Xiaoshuai Zhang; Xingru Huang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133602

Relevance: 0.738 (ignore)

Accurate 3D medical image segmentation remains challenging for anisotropic clinical volumes and fine anatomical structures, where conventional encoder-decoder sampling often causes aliasing, boundary attenuation, and unstable detail reconstruction. In this work, we argue that the bottleneck is not only representation capacity, but also how information is discarded and reconstructed across scales. We propose SVR-UNet, a frequency-aware and view-routed U-shaped network that reformulates downsampling and upsampling as an explicit analysis-synthesis process. Specifically, we introduce Trihedral Spectro-Projective Decimation (TSPD) and Trihedral Spectro-Projective Prolongation (TSPP) as sampling modules that adapt established ideas from anti-aliased filtering, wavelet multiresolution analysis, and orthogonal-view modeling to encoder–decoder resolution transitions. TSPD performs anti-aliased decimation, tri-view projective decomposition, Haar wavelet subband modeling, and gradient-prior view routing to preserve boundary-critical high-frequency content during encoding. TSPP performs controlled spectral synthesis during decoding by combining tri-view wavelet-lifting reconstruction with edge-confidence-guided fusion, enabling anatomically faithful recovery of thin and weak-contrast structures while suppressing upsampling artifacts. Extensive experiments on 10 public 3D medical image segmentation benchmarks against 26 recent state-of-the-art (SoTA) methods show that SVR-UNet obtains competitive overall results and improves several boundary-oriented metrics, while maintaining a compact footprint of 16.88M parameters and 166.99G FLOPs. The results suggest that frequency-aware, view-routed sampling can serve as a useful complement to backbone scaling for volumetric segmentation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/IMOP-lab/SVR-UNet .

item.title: Performance evaluation of a proposed two stage cascaded convolutional neural network architecture in image classification
item.dc:creator: Isuru Bandara Samarakoon
Haider Abbas Almurib
Anandan Shanmugam
item.description: Convolutional neural network models have been developed over the past few decades demonstrating remarkable performance in image classification tasks for various applications in industries. The potential to produce higher accuracy has resulted in deep convolutional neural networks widely used in research where the presence of complex computations is the secret of success. Larger complex computations cause an increased inference time for a single classification, which eventually leads to overall latency of vision-based systems. This research work focuses on minimizing the inference time of a convolutional neural network while achieving better classification accuracy by cascading modified convolutional neural network models. Learnable layers of a CNN, such as convolutional and fully connected layers, are involved in heavy complex computations that account for most of the inference time. Modified architecture with optimized computations while maintaining generalization capability leads to minimizing latency. The cascaded neural network model trained on a lesser number of output classes offered better classification accuracies and reduced inference time compared to standard convolutional neural network models such as Alex net. The performance of the cascaded neural network model was tested for classification accuracy and execution time for a wide range of test data presented, that proposed cascaded network inference speed was enhanced by 6% while maintaining the classification accuracy as the AlexNet for tests conducted on 200 object classes. Published: 2026-07-11T02:40:46+00:00 Venue: Neural Computing and Applications Score: 0.737 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Isuru Bandara Samarakoon; Haider Abbas Almurib; Anandan Shanmugam

Journal: Neural Computing and Applications

DOI: 10.1007/s00521-026-12240-7

Relevance: 0.737 (ignore)

Convolutional neural network models have been developed over the past few decades demonstrating remarkable performance in image classification tasks for various applications in industries. The potential to produce higher accuracy has resulted in deep convolutional neural networks widely used in research where the presence of complex computations is the secret of success. Larger complex computations cause an increased inference time for a single classification, which eventually leads to overall latency of vision-based systems. This research work focuses on minimizing the inference time of a convolutional neural network while achieving better classification accuracy by cascading modified convolutional neural network models. Learnable layers of a CNN, such as convolutional and fully connected layers, are involved in heavy complex computations that account for most of the inference time. Modified architecture with optimized computations while maintaining generalization capability leads to minimizing latency. The cascaded neural network model trained on a lesser number of output classes offered better classification accuracies and reduced inference time compared to standard convolutional neural network models such as Alex net. The performance of the cascaded neural network model was tested for classification accuracy and execution time for a wide range of test data presented, that proposed cascaded network inference speed was enhanced by 6% while maintaining the classification accuracy as the AlexNet for tests conducted on 200 object classes.

item.title: Dynamic tokenization in the transformer era: A review and taxonomy
item.dc:creator: Mohammad Mahdi Azizi
Bagher BabaAli
Fatemeh Ziaeetabar
item.description: Tokenization—the process of converting raw input into discrete units for Transformer-based models—has long relied on static, modality-specific strategies such as fixed patching in vision or subword segmentation in language. While computationally convenient, these methods often suffer from semantic misalignment, inefficiency, and limited adaptability across diverse or noisy real-world data. In response, a growing body of work has explored dynamic tokenization, a paradigm inspired by human cognitive chunking, wherein token boundaries, cardinality, and representations are determined adaptively based on input content. This review provides a comprehensive synthesis of dynamic tokenization in Transformers across modalities. Drawing on a systematic analysis of 76 studies across vision, language, audio, time series, and modality-agnostic settings, we propose a unified two-axis taxonomy—defined by what guides token formation (perceptual, task-driven, or hybrid) and by how tokens map to the input (aligned or unaligned)—to categorize and compare design philosophies. Across these studies, we identify recurring evidence that dynamic tokenization can mitigate key limitations of static methods by improving semantic fidelity, computational efficiency, and robustness, while also revealing persistent trade-offs among interpretability, generality, and scalability. We further identify critical gaps in evaluation and architectural integration, and outline a roadmap toward foundation models that treat tokenization not as a fixed pipeline, but as a learnable mechanism of adaptive perception. By reframing tokens as dynamic, context-sensitive units, this paradigm represents a promising direction toward more efficient, equitable, and cognitively inspired AI systems. Published: 2026-07-11T14:52:05+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.735 (ignore)

While computationally convenient, these methods often suffer from semantic misalignment, inefficiency, and limited adaptability across diverse or noisy real-world data. In response, a growing body of work has explored dynamic tokenization, a paradigm inspired by human cognitive chunking, wherein token boundaries, cardinality, and representations are determined adaptively based on input content. Drawing on a systematic analysis of 76 studies across vision, language, audio, time series, and modality-agnostic settings, we propose a unified two-axis taxonomy—defined by what guides token formation (perceptual, task-driven, or hybrid) and by how tokens map to the input (aligned or unaligned)—to categorize and compare design philosophies. Across these studies, we identify recurring evidence that dynamic tokenization can mitigate key limitations of static methods by improving semantic fidelity, computational efficiency, and robustness, while also revealing persistent trade-offs among interpretability, generality, and scalability. We further identify critical gaps in evaluation and architectural integration, and outline a roadmap toward foundation models that treat tokenization not as a fixed pipeline, but as a learnable mechanism of adaptive perception. By reframing tokens as dynamic, context-sensitive units, this paradigm represents a promising direction toward more efficient, equitable, and cognitively inspired AI systems.

item.title: The muzzle as a biometric feature: Cattle identification using unique muzzle patterns
item.dc:creator: Tomáš Goldmann
Samuel Šimún
Filip Orság
item.description: Cattle identification often suffers from domain-induced bias and closed-set limitations. This paper introduces a rigorous open-set verification benchmark for cattle muzzle biometrics, explicitly designed to mitigate domain-induced bias. We conduct an extensive ablation study evaluating the interplay between architecture (CNNs vs. Vision Transformers), input resolution, and modern angular margin loss functions, including ArcFace, CosFace, and AdaFace. Our findings reveal a resolution-dependent architectural divergence: at low resolutions (256 × 192), keypoint-based alignment boosts standard CNNs to 95.45% accuracy (VGG11-BN with ArcFace). However, at higher resolutions (384 × 384), we observe that while the Swin Transformer requires explicit alignment to achieve 96.54% accuracy, modern CNNs (e.g., MobileNetV3-L) achieve superior performance (96.14%) on unaligned crops. Importantly, our results suggest that even with the limited data available in this domain, Transformers can effectively capture muzzle features, although their sensitivity to alignment is architecture-dependent. It is pronounced for the Swin Transformer, yet absent for DeiT and modern CNNs, which remain robust to unaligned crops. Published: 2026-07-11T06:33:18+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.733 (ignore)

Authors: Tomáš Goldmann; Samuel Šimún; Filip Orság

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114417

Relevance: 0.733 (ignore)

Cattle identification often suffers from domain-induced bias and closed-set limitations. This paper introduces a rigorous open-set verification benchmark for cattle muzzle biometrics, explicitly designed to mitigate domain-induced bias. We conduct an extensive ablation study evaluating the interplay between architecture (CNNs vs. Vision Transformers), input resolution, and modern angular margin loss functions, including ArcFace, CosFace, and AdaFace. Our findings reveal a resolution-dependent architectural divergence: at low resolutions (256 × 192), keypoint-based alignment boosts standard CNNs to 95.45% accuracy (VGG11-BN with ArcFace). However, at higher resolutions (384 × 384), we observe that while the Swin Transformer requires explicit alignment to achieve 96.54% accuracy, modern CNNs (e.g., MobileNetV3-L) achieve superior performance (96.14%) on unaligned crops. Importantly, our results suggest that even with the limited data available in this domain, Transformers can effectively capture muzzle features, although their sensitivity to alignment is architecture-dependent. It is pronounced for the Swin Transformer, yet absent for DeiT and modern CNNs, which remain robust to unaligned crops.

item.title: A continuous-time generative model for irregular and regular industrial time series
item.dc:creator: Yakun Wang
Yisheng Zou
Gang Wang
Sonlin He
Linfu Sun
Ning Zhou
item.description: Generating synthetic industrial time series data is critical for enhancing the performance of industrial systems. However, in complex industrial environments, real-world time series data often suffer from issues such as missing values and irregular sampling caused by sensor failures and delayed data acquisition, making the task of synthesizing high-quality data particularly challenging. Existing methods, due to their inherently discrete nature, struggle to capture the continuous and complex temporal dynamics present in irregular time systems. To address these limitations, we propose ContiSyn, a novel industrial time series generation model designed to support the modeling of continuous-time dynamics in both regular and irregular time series. Specifically, ContiSyn first extracts continuous temporal features from discrete observations to capture the underlying complex temporal dependencies within the data. It then employs an adversarial training strategy to enhance the realism and fidelity of the generated sequences. Furthermore, ContiSyn incorporates the log-density of hidden states as an auxiliary loss to jointly model the global temporal dynamics and the latent distribution of time series data. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple real-world industrial datasets demonstrate that ContiSyn consistently generates synthetic sequences that closely resemble observed data in both statistical properties and temporal structure, clearly outperforming existing baseline methods. Published: 2026-07-11T14:52:05+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.731 (ignore)
