Authors: Yakun Wang; Yisheng Zou; Gang Wang; Sonlin He; Linfu Sun; Ning Zhou

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114429

Relevance: 0.731 (ignore)

Generating synthetic industrial time series data is critical for enhancing the performance of industrial systems. However, in complex industrial environments, real-world time series data often suffer from issues such as missing values and irregular sampling caused by sensor failures and delayed data acquisition, making the task of synthesizing high-quality data particularly challenging. Existing methods, due to their inherently discrete nature, struggle to capture the continuous and complex temporal dynamics present in irregular time systems. To address these limitations, we propose ContiSyn, a novel industrial time series generation model designed to support the modeling of continuous-time dynamics in both regular and irregular time series. Specifically, ContiSyn first extracts continuous temporal features from discrete observations to capture the underlying complex temporal dependencies within the data. It then employs an adversarial training strategy to enhance the realism and fidelity of the generated sequences. Furthermore, ContiSyn incorporates the log-density of hidden states as an auxiliary loss to jointly model the global temporal dynamics and the latent distribution of time series data. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple real-world industrial datasets demonstrate that ContiSyn consistently generates synthetic sequences that closely resemble observed data in both statistical properties and temporal structure, clearly outperforming existing baseline methods.

item.title: FoodXLLM : An explainable deep learning-based food recommendation system
item.dc:creator: Ephraim Sinyabe Pagou
Corneille Vivient Kamla
Josiane Ngathic
Igor Tchappi
item.description: Food Recommender Systems (FRS) based on Deep Learning (DL) effectively predict user preferences using large amounts of structured and unstructured data. However, most architectures operate as “black boxes” due to limited or unintuitive explanations. Existing methods often rely on feature attribution or post-hoc counterfactual reasoning, which may fail to provide user-friendly justifications. Integrating explainability into FRS remains a significant challenge. This paper introduces an explainability module for a DL-based FRS using Large Language Models (LLMs), which leverage vast textual knowledge to understand and generate human language and context. Our approach incorporates LLMs to enrich the original dataset, enabling more nuanced interpretations of dietary recommendations by better capturing relationships between users, recipes, and user-recipe interactions. Using these relationships, the FRS inputs and outputs are then used to generate explanations for its recommendations. Experiments on two large-scale benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the explainable DL-based FRS. The RMSE values achieved are 0.5985 and 0.3179, outperforming DL-based FRS without explainability. These results highlight the importance of integrating explainability into FRS to improve both predictive accuracy and interpretability. Published: 2026-07-11T06:37:24+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.728 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ephraim Sinyabe Pagou; Corneille Vivient Kamla; Josiane Ngathic; Igor Tchappi

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116615

Relevance: 0.728 (ignore)

Food Recommender Systems (FRS) based on Deep Learning (DL) effectively predict user preferences using large amounts of structured and unstructured data. However, most architectures operate as “black boxes” due to limited or unintuitive explanations. Existing methods often rely on feature attribution or post-hoc counterfactual reasoning, which may fail to provide user-friendly justifications. Integrating explainability into FRS remains a significant challenge. This paper introduces an explainability module for a DL-based FRS using Large Language Models (LLMs), which leverage vast textual knowledge to understand and generate human language and context. Our approach incorporates LLMs to enrich the original dataset, enabling more nuanced interpretations of dietary recommendations by better capturing relationships between users, recipes, and user-recipe interactions. Using these relationships, the FRS inputs and outputs are then used to generate explanations for its recommendations. Experiments on two large-scale benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the explainable DL-based FRS. The RMSE values achieved are 0.5985 and 0.3179, outperforming DL-based FRS without explainability. These results highlight the importance of integrating explainability into FRS to improve both predictive accuracy and interpretability.

item.title: Enhancing large language models for knowledge-intensive question answering with dual-path knowledge integration
item.dc:creator: Jikui Wang
Kaixuan Guo
Yonggang Zhang
item.description: Large Language Models achieve impressive performance across various NLP tasks but often struggle in knowledge-intensive reasoning scenarios due to the lack of explicit grounding. Structured knowledge graphs offer a promising external knowledge source, but existing integration methods are often limited by noisy subgraphs and semantic misalignment between symbolic structures and language representations. We propose a coordinated dual-path knowledge integration framework that integrates input-level enhancement with parameter-level knowledge injection, addressing the limitations of single-path methods in both selecting relevant knowledge and effectively integrating it into model representations. Specifically, our approach dynamically filters noisy triples via a weighted subgraph selection mechanism, aligns textual and graph semantics through attention-based fine-grained matching, and injects KG representations into the LLM’s parameter space to provide structural guidance during inference. In parallel, subgraph-derived soft prompts enhance input embeddings, conditioning the representation space and improving the effectiveness of subsequent knowledge injection. Experiments on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in reasoning accuracy, supporting the effectiveness of the proposed dual-path integration strategy. Published: 2026-07-11T06:36:54+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.728 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jikui Wang; Kaixuan Guo; Yonggang Zhang

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134477

Relevance: 0.728 (ignore)

Large Language Models achieve impressive performance across various NLP tasks but often struggle in knowledge-intensive reasoning scenarios due to the lack of explicit grounding. Structured knowledge graphs offer a promising external knowledge source, but existing integration methods are often limited by noisy subgraphs and semantic misalignment between symbolic structures and language representations. We propose a coordinated dual-path knowledge integration framework that integrates input-level enhancement with parameter-level knowledge injection, addressing the limitations of single-path methods in both selecting relevant knowledge and effectively integrating it into model representations. Specifically, our approach dynamically filters noisy triples via a weighted subgraph selection mechanism, aligns textual and graph semantics through attention-based fine-grained matching, and injects KG representations into the LLM’s parameter space to provide structural guidance during inference. In parallel, subgraph-derived soft prompts enhance input embeddings, conditioning the representation space and improving the effectiveness of subsequent knowledge injection. Experiments on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in reasoning accuracy, supporting the effectiveness of the proposed dual-path integration strategy.

item.title: ProTeCt: Geometry-Aware Reference-Guided Video Semantic Segmentation for Event Camera APS Frames
item.dc:creator: Zhuoxian Li
Hengyi Lv
Xiangzhi Li
Ming Sun
Cheng Peng
Junyao Sang
item.description: Robust perception during event-stream failures—termed “Degraded Perception Mode”—is a critical safety requirement for event-based vision systems. While Active Pixel Sensor (APS) frames provide essential fallback context, they suffer from a “Dual Dilemma”: spectral intensity degeneracy (e.g., indistinguishable road vs. sidewalk textures) and exposure-induced motion blur. To address this, we propose ProTeCt, a Reference-Guided Video Semantic Segmentation framework that leverages sparse high-quality anchors for perceptual calibration. First, we introduce a Coordinate-Aware Structured Prototype Interaction Module (C-PIM), which projects pixels into a joint geometry-semantic manifold to resolve grayscale ambiguities via spatial invariants. Second, a Temporal Reliability Gating (TRG) mechanism replaces vulnerable optical flow with a reliability-aware “circuit breaker,” adaptively suppressing blur propagation while reconstructing features via reference prototypes.Finally, a Geometric Equivariance-guided Prototype Evolution strategy is proposed to prevent memory contamination by enforcing self-supervised consistency constraints during online adaptation. Extensive experiments on DSEC-Semantic and DDD17 demonstrate that ProTeCt significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, establishing a resilient standard for event camera perception under bandwidth-constrained conditions. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Protect-8AD7 . Published: 2026-07-11T14:58:57+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.726 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhuoxian Li; Hengyi Lv; Xiangzhi Li; Ming Sun; Cheng Peng; Junyao Sang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133531

Relevance: 0.726 (ignore)

Robust perception during event-stream failures—termed “Degraded Perception Mode”—is a critical safety requirement for event-based vision systems. While Active Pixel Sensor (APS) frames provide essential fallback context, they suffer from a “Dual Dilemma”: spectral intensity degeneracy (e.g., indistinguishable road vs. sidewalk textures) and exposure-induced motion blur. To address this, we propose ProTeCt, a Reference-Guided Video Semantic Segmentation framework that leverages sparse high-quality anchors for perceptual calibration. First, we introduce a Coordinate-Aware Structured Prototype Interaction Module (C-PIM), which projects pixels into a joint geometry-semantic manifold to resolve grayscale ambiguities via spatial invariants. Second, a Temporal Reliability Gating (TRG) mechanism replaces vulnerable optical flow with a reliability-aware “circuit breaker,” adaptively suppressing blur propagation while reconstructing features via reference prototypes.Finally, a Geometric Equivariance-guided Prototype Evolution strategy is proposed to prevent memory contamination by enforcing self-supervised consistency constraints during online adaptation. Extensive experiments on DSEC-Semantic and DDD17 demonstrate that ProTeCt significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, establishing a resilient standard for event camera perception under bandwidth-constrained conditions. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Protect-8AD7 .

item.title: A new subspace minimization conjugate gradient algorithm without explicit use of gradient terms
item.dc:creator: Lihui Peng
Yihuai Xu
Gan Xiao
Bingshuo Wu
Yihe Rui
item.description: Motivated by the Nazareth conjugate gradient method and the subspace minimization technique, we propose a new subspace minimization conjugate gradient algorithm. This method constructs a subspace using the previous search direction and gradient differences across three consecutive iterates, avoiding the direct use of the current gradient. Within this subspace, a quadratic approximation problem is solved to determine the subsequent search direction, and the global convergence result is established under standard Wolfe line search conditions. Numerical experiments demonstrate that, compared to existing methods such as CG_DESCENT and CGOPT, the proposed algorithm solves a higher percentage of problems with fewer iterations, reduced CPU runtime, and fewer function and gradient evaluations; and in the salt-and-pepper noise removal experiment, the proposed method achieves restoration quality comparable to baseline methods while incurring significantly lower optimization costs, highlighting its robustness and efficiency. Published: 2026-07-11T14:50:49+00:00 Venue: Information Sciences Score: 0.721 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lihui Peng; Yihuai Xu; Gan Xiao; Bingshuo Wu; Yihe Rui

Journal: Information Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.ins.2026.123894

Relevance: 0.721 (ignore)

Motivated by the Nazareth conjugate gradient method and the subspace minimization technique, we propose a new subspace minimization conjugate gradient algorithm. This method constructs a subspace using the previous search direction and gradient differences across three consecutive iterates, avoiding the direct use of the current gradient. Within this subspace, a quadratic approximation problem is solved to determine the subsequent search direction, and the global convergence result is established under standard Wolfe line search conditions. Numerical experiments demonstrate that, compared to existing methods such as CG_DESCENT and CGOPT, the proposed algorithm solves a higher percentage of problems with fewer iterations, reduced CPU runtime, and fewer function and gradient evaluations; and in the salt-and-pepper noise removal experiment, the proposed method achieves restoration quality comparable to baseline methods while incurring significantly lower optimization costs, highlighting its robustness and efficiency.

item.title: Decoupled dual-branch prototype network with fine-grained context mining for self–supervised few–shot abdominal image segmentation
item.dc:creator: Di Gai
Yuxuan Zou
Fan Luo
Jieying Tu
Yuhan Geng
Dengyun Xu
Pengxiang Su
item.description: Recently, deep learning-based segmentation methodologies heavily depend on large-scale annotated datasets that require experienced medical experts to ensure integrity and accuracy, which poses a significant obstacle to progress in the field of medical imaging. Most existing studies employ prototype networks to address the limited data volume and have obtained convincing performance in the few-shot segmentation (FSS) domain. However, the previous methods ignore potential correlation features between foreground and background, failing to sufficiently extract detailed and implicit features of organs in medical images. In this paper, we propose a novel two-branch (the edge-aware refinement branch and the context-guided semantic branch) self-supervised FSS framework, including the deformable edge-aware attention module (DEAM) and the hierarchical context interaction module (HCIM). Furthermore, the dual-branch structure learns different levels of feature representation, by fusing low-level image edges with high-level semantic features to capture the complexity and potential semantic information. The edge-aware refinement branch employs DEAM within the prototype network to address the foreground-background imbalance, while the context-guided semantic branch utilizes HCIM to acquire fine-grained features from various organs. Extensive experiments on abdominal MRI and abdominal CT datasets demonstrate our approach achieves significant improvements against existing state-of-the-art methods. Published: 2026-07-11T06:34:35+00:00 Venue: Image and Vision Computing Score: 0.720 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Di Gai; Yuxuan Zou; Fan Luo; Jieying Tu; Yuhan Geng; Dengyun Xu; Pengxiang Su

Journal: Image and Vision Computing

DOI: 10.1016/j.imavis.2026.106102

Relevance: 0.720 (ignore)

Recently, deep learning-based segmentation methodologies heavily depend on large-scale annotated datasets that require experienced medical experts to ensure integrity and accuracy, which poses a significant obstacle to progress in the field of medical imaging. Most existing studies employ prototype networks to address the limited data volume and have obtained convincing performance in the few-shot segmentation (FSS) domain. However, the previous methods ignore potential correlation features between foreground and background, failing to sufficiently extract detailed and implicit features of organs in medical images. In this paper, we propose a novel two-branch (the edge-aware refinement branch and the context-guided semantic branch) self-supervised FSS framework, including the deformable edge-aware attention module (DEAM) and the hierarchical context interaction module (HCIM). Furthermore, the dual-branch structure learns different levels of feature representation, by fusing low-level image edges with high-level semantic features to capture the complexity and potential semantic information. The edge-aware refinement branch employs DEAM within the prototype network to address the foreground-background imbalance, while the context-guided semantic branch utilizes HCIM to acquire fine-grained features from various organs. Extensive experiments on abdominal MRI and abdominal CT datasets demonstrate our approach achieves significant improvements against existing state-of-the-art methods.

item.title: EmoSkeMPR: ViT-based masked position reconstruction and skeleton feature fusion for multi-task emotion and behavior recognition
item.dc:creator: Xianlin Peng
Lingjie Kong
Qiyao Hu
Jinye Peng
Gu Fang
item.description: Core social emotion and behavior state analysis of wild Qinling golden monkeys has value for ethological analysis, but severe natural occlusions often obscure key facial and torso features. Existing methods struggle with structural distortions, semantic ambiguity, and poor physical topology modeling, leading to feature decoupling failures in joint core social emotion and behavior recognition. To overcome this, we propose EmoSkeMPR, a multi-task framework synergistically guided by masked position reconstruction and skeleton features. First, a Spatial Occlusion Perception Module (SOPM) strips irregular environmental noise. Second, a Semantic Prior Extraction Module (SPEM) injects global semantic knowledge into a Mask Feature Reconstruction Module (MFRM) to reconstruct missing regions with high fidelity. Finally, a Skeleton-Synergistic Multi-task Module (SSMM) aligns visual features with fine-tuned HRNet skeleton features. Using skeleton topology as a physical reference, SSMM decouples shared features to synchronously predict core social emotions and behavior. Furthermore, we construct GM-MTL, a new multi-task dataset with multi-dimensional hierarchical annotations. Experiments demonstrate that EmoSkeMPR outperforms the compared state-of-the-art models in visual restoration fidelity and multi-task classification accuracy, providing a reference framework for wild primate monitoring. Published: 2026-07-11T14:50:44+00:00 Venue: Information Sciences Score: 0.719 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xianlin Peng; Lingjie Kong; Qiyao Hu; Jinye Peng; Gu Fang

Journal: Information Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.ins.2026.123895

Relevance: 0.719 (ignore)

Core social emotion and behavior state analysis of wild Qinling golden monkeys has value for ethological analysis, but severe natural occlusions often obscure key facial and torso features. Existing methods struggle with structural distortions, semantic ambiguity, and poor physical topology modeling, leading to feature decoupling failures in joint core social emotion and behavior recognition. To overcome this, we propose EmoSkeMPR, a multi-task framework synergistically guided by masked position reconstruction and skeleton features. First, a Spatial Occlusion Perception Module (SOPM) strips irregular environmental noise. Second, a Semantic Prior Extraction Module (SPEM) injects global semantic knowledge into a Mask Feature Reconstruction Module (MFRM) to reconstruct missing regions with high fidelity. Finally, a Skeleton-Synergistic Multi-task Module (SSMM) aligns visual features with fine-tuned HRNet skeleton features. Using skeleton topology as a physical reference, SSMM decouples shared features to synchronously predict core social emotions and behavior. Furthermore, we construct GM-MTL, a new multi-task dataset with multi-dimensional hierarchical annotations. Experiments demonstrate that EmoSkeMPR outperforms the compared state-of-the-art models in visual restoration fidelity and multi-task classification accuracy, providing a reference framework for wild primate monitoring.

item.title: A reinforcement learning framework for network dismantling under defense constraints
item.dc:creator: Ke Liu
Feng Qing
Fengwei Guo
Ling Zhang
Chao Chen
Jincai Huang
Zhong Liu
Changjun Fan
item.description: Complex network dismantling is strategically important for combating harmful networks like terrorist organizations. However, certain nodes within harmful networks may be protected. Failure to account for this may cause attackers to expend substantial resources while remaining unable to remove them. We define this issue as “network dismantling under defense constraints”. Regarding this issue, existing studies predominantly focus on equilibrium strategies based on attack-defense games, yet research on identifying effective dismantling strategies for networks with unknown defended nodes remains scarce. Here, we introduce a framework for network dismantling under defense constraints based on reinforcement learning. This framework establishes an interactive environment between heuristic defense strategies and design models, enabling the learning of effective attack strategies via defense-aware reward signals to dismantle networks with unknown defended nodes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms baselines on synthetic and real-world networks under different simulated defense mechanisms in most cases. This study provides an efficient solution for network dismantling under defense constraints, holding great significance for harmful-network dismantling. Published: 2026-07-11T06:36:48+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.718 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ke Liu; Feng Qing; Fengwei Guo; Ling Zhang; Chao Chen; Jincai Huang; Zhong Liu; Changjun Fan

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134474

Relevance: 0.718 (ignore)

Complex network dismantling is strategically important for combating harmful networks like terrorist organizations. However, certain nodes within harmful networks may be protected. Failure to account for this may cause attackers to expend substantial resources while remaining unable to remove them. We define this issue as “network dismantling under defense constraints”. Regarding this issue, existing studies predominantly focus on equilibrium strategies based on attack-defense games, yet research on identifying effective dismantling strategies for networks with unknown defended nodes remains scarce. Here, we introduce a framework for network dismantling under defense constraints based on reinforcement learning. This framework establishes an interactive environment between heuristic defense strategies and design models, enabling the learning of effective attack strategies via defense-aware reward signals to dismantle networks with unknown defended nodes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms baselines on synthetic and real-world networks under different simulated defense mechanisms in most cases. This study provides an efficient solution for network dismantling under defense constraints, holding great significance for harmful-network dismantling.

item.title: Multivariate time series forecasting with a numerical spiking neural p system-inspired network
item.dc:creator: Jiachang Xu
Jiahao Pan
Shuzhi Su
Hongjin Li
Ruijuan Zhao
Fei Ding
item.description: Inspired by Membrane Computing, this paper addresses a core challenge in Multivariate Time Series Forecasting (MTSF): extracting robust intra-series dynamics while mitigating cross-variable noise. Existing membrane-based models often lack comprehensive validation against state-of-the-art (SOTA) deep learning methods. To bridge this gap, we propose the Numerical Spiking Neural P System-Inspired Network (NSNP-Net), an end-to-end deep learning architecture integrating the distributed parallel mechanisms of P systems. First, the abstract “production function” is instantiated as learnable temporal pattern vectors to capture univariate dynamics. Second, to enhance representation without introducing cross-channel noise, we design a cyclic channel permutation and multi-step structural ensemble mechanism for robust prediction fusion. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that NSNP-Net consistently achieves or surpasses SOTA performance. This study not only introduces a new high-performance model for time series forecasting but also builds an innovative bridge between membrane computing and deep learning, validating an effective new paradigm for empowering concrete applications with abstract theories. Published: 2026-07-11T14:58:13+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.711 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiachang Xu; Jiahao Pan; Shuzhi Su; Hongjin Li; Ruijuan Zhao; Fei Ding

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134476

Relevance: 0.711 (ignore)

Inspired by Membrane Computing, this paper addresses a core challenge in Multivariate Time Series Forecasting (MTSF): extracting robust intra-series dynamics while mitigating cross-variable noise. Existing membrane-based models often lack comprehensive validation against state-of-the-art (SOTA) deep learning methods. To bridge this gap, we propose the Numerical Spiking Neural P System-Inspired Network (NSNP-Net), an end-to-end deep learning architecture integrating the distributed parallel mechanisms of P systems. First, the abstract “production function” is instantiated as learnable temporal pattern vectors to capture univariate dynamics. Second, to enhance representation without introducing cross-channel noise, we design a cyclic channel permutation and multi-step structural ensemble mechanism for robust prediction fusion. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that NSNP-Net consistently achieves or surpasses SOTA performance. This study not only introduces a new high-performance model for time series forecasting but also builds an innovative bridge between membrane computing and deep learning, validating an effective new paradigm for empowering concrete applications with abstract theories.
